text.title: selling-ideas Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for selling-ideas. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Never pitch the solution until Implication questions have made the problem feel urgent to the audience.
rule.rationale: Rackham's research on 35,000 sales calls showed that Implication questions — not product descriptions — are the primary driver of decision-maker movement. Features presented before pain is amplified produce "that's interesting" responses, not decisions.

rule.statement: Use the Teach-Tailor-Take-Control sequence when the audience does not yet know they have a problem: share a surprising insight, connect it to their situation, then guide the decision.
rule.rationale: Challengers win by reframing the buyer's understanding of their own problem. Mere problem-solvers compete on features; Challengers compete on perspective.

rule.statement: Use the 7-line elevator pitch template to ensure every pitch covers: target customer, problem, category, key benefit, competitor contrast, and differentiator.
rule.rationale: Omitting any element produces a pitch that either confuses the audience about who this is for (no target), fails to establish urgency (no problem), or cannot be remembered (no differentiator).

rule.statement: Match pitch structure to the audience role: executives need business impact first, technical audiences need architecture before benefits, finance needs cost savings with payback period.
rule.rationale: Each role has a primary decision filter. Leading with the wrong frame (e.g., technical architecture to an executive) triggers disengagement before the key point lands.

rule.statement: Use LAER (Listen, Acknowledge, Explore, Respond) for every objection — never skip Explore before Responding.
rule.rationale: Responding before exploring treats the stated objection as the real one. The real objection is usually different: "too expensive" often means "I don't see the ROI" not "we have no budget".

rule.statement: Structure presentations as: Hook → Problem → Solution → Proof → Call to Action. End with a specific ask, never an open-ended close.
rule.rationale: Presentations without a clear ask produce "let me think about it" deferrals. The ask defines what decision the audience is making.

rule.statement: Always lead with the Problem, not the Solution — agents default to solution-first because specs are usually solution-first; explicitly reverse the order in the prompt.
rule.rationale: SPIN question sequences need authentic human delivery; agent-written SPIN scripts read as interrogative rather than curious.

rule.statement: The Challenger Sale "Teach" step requires genuinely novel industry insight — agents will produce plausible-sounding but often generic "insights" that sophisticated buyers will recognize as boilerplate.
rule.rationale: Objection handling scripts generated without real objection data tend to address imagined concerns, not actual ones.

rule.statement: Quantification ("40% lower TCO") requires real data; agents will hallucinate specifics if not given source numbers.
rule.rationale: Agents will invent specific percentages when not given real data — always mark claims as [DATA NEEDED] in the prompt and review before use.

rule.statement: Build an objection library over time: after each pitch, log real objections raised and effective responses. Feed this library into future agent prompts.
rule.rationale: For executive pitches: one metric per slide, no more. An agent will fill space with data; trim ruthlessly.

rule.statement: The Elevator Pitch 10-second version ("We help [WHO] [DO WHAT] [BETTER/FASTER/CHEAPER]") is the hardest to write well and the most important; dedicate a separate prompt pass to it.
rule.rationale: SPIN sequences work best when the Implication questions quantify cost ("How much engineering time does a rollback cost?") — ensure the brief includes actual cost data, even rough estimates.

rule.statement: "Ask" statements generated by agents are often weak ("Let's schedule a follow-up") — specify what type of commitment you want (time / budget / referral) in the prompt.
rule.rationale: Power-phrase substitutions (Weak → Strong) can sound aggressive if applied wholesale; human review needed to calibrate tone per audience.

rule.statement: SPIN question sequences generated without audience context will produce Situation questions that are too broad and Implication questions too generic to create urgency.
rule.rationale: Live negotiation cannot be handled by agents that cannot adapt to in-room dynamics in real time; agents can prepare but not participate live.
